Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to sit down and study, but I learn best by doing things with my hands, so just reading or watching videos felt really draining. I'd usually end up studying in bursts when I could physically practice something or work through problems, but the routine itself was never consistent. With limited resources, I often had to make do with whatever time and materials I could scrape together.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was breathing down my neck. Being 27 and dealing with life stuff means I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule. The anxiety about falling behind would build up, but somehow I'd still procrastinate until the pressure was unbearable.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or that gnawing fear that I'd missed something important. Sometimes a peer would message me asking about an assignment, and that would prompt me to log in and check what was happening. I rarely logged in just to explore or browse around—it was almost always reactive, driven by necessity rather than curiosity.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd go through phases where I logged in more regularly, especially after peer support sessions made me feel like I should be more on top of things. But that routine never stuck for long—life would get in the way, or my anxiety would make me avoid it altogether.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually helpful when I could get myself to participate, because hearing from other students made concepts click better for me. I also found the videos useful when I could actually focus, though 89 minutes of watching doesn't mean I absorbed everything. Anything that felt interactive or connected to real application worked better for my kinesthetic learning style.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They just felt overwhelming, and when you're already anxious, a huge PDF feels like a mountain. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came. The optional stuff especially got pushed aside because my brain couldn't handle anything beyond the bare minimum most days.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to passing the course or understanding something I'd actually use. If it felt abstract or disconnected, my brain just checked out. I needed to feel like there was a tangible outcome—like completing it would actually move me forward rather than just being busywork.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. Required ones came with consequences, so even when I was scared and overwhelmed, I'd force myself through them eventually. Optional stuff? My anxiety would whisper that I should focus on what's mandatory first, and then I'd never circle back. There was always something more pressing.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download materials and study them on my phone during commutes or when I didn't have reliable internet access. I also borrowed textbooks from friends and took handwritten notes because writing things out helps me remember better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems on paper, physically practicing concepts, or discussing material with peers in person. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also re-read my own notes multiple times before exams—that's studying, but it's completely invisible to the platform.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading was practical. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own before I felt I understood it.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. When I felt scared about falling behind, ironically, I'd avoid the course entirely because facing it felt too overwhelming. Financial stress and personal responsibilities also pulled my attention away from academics during certain weeks.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from other responsibilities, the LMS became something I'd dread opening. Confusion about what was expected would paralyze me—I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by all the tasks, and log out without doing anything meaningful. My low resilience meant I didn't bounce back easily from those moments.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open materials, scroll through them, maybe watch a video on 2x speed, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It was like being physically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt difficult or when my anxiety was high. The bigger or more important something seemed, the more scared I felt, and the more I'd avoid it. I knew it was self-sabotage, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope with the fear.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd cram everything into the last possible window, which was stressful but somehow effective enough to get decent scores. The LMS would probably show these spikes of activity right before due dates. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback would send my anxiety through the roof, and I'd avoid that assignment or topic for a while. The peer support helped buffer some of that—having others encourage me made feedback feel less personal and scary.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid, which was both good and bad. Good because it gave me clear expectations, bad because when I fell behind, catching up felt impossible. There wasn't much flexibility for someone like me who sometimes needs to step back and approach things differently due to anxiety.
Q19: Having everything in one place on the LMS was helpful, but the sheer amount of content was intimidating. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me focus. The discussion forums were a nice touch because they made me feel less alone, which eased some of my fear.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in frequently out of anxiety, checking things obsessively, but then being too overwhelmed or scared to actually complete and submit work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just nervous energy that doesn't translate into output.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I performed reasonably well despite low LMS activity because a lot of my real studying happened offline. I'd work through things on paper, discuss with peers in person, and only use the LMS when absolutely necessary. The platform didn't capture the full picture of my effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the emotional state behind the numbers. They can't tell if someone is anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused for a short time.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the people in the course. For me, it's when I'm not just going through the motions but genuinely trying to understand and apply things. It's also about feeling like you belong and aren't drowning alone.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how peer support sessions genuinely lifted my mood and helped me keep going. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the anxiety-driven avoidance, or the moments when a simple encouraging message from a classmate made all the difference. The human stuff is invisible to the data.
